metadata.title: Azure Security Engineer AZ-500 Practice Questions

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of Azure AD Privileged Identity Management (PIM)?
options: Provide just-in-time privileged access to Azure AD and Azure resources
Manage user passwords and password reset policies
Configure multi-factor authentication for all users
Monitor network traffic between Azure virtual networks
questions.explanation: Azure AD PIM provides just-in-time privileged access, allowing users to activate eligible role assignments only when needed, reducing the risk of standing privileged access.

questions.question: Which type of managed identity is automatically deleted when the associated Azure resource is deleted?
options: System-assigned managed identity
User-assigned managed identity
Service principal identity
Application identity
questions.explanation: A system-assigned managed identity is tied to the lifecycle of the Azure resource. When the resource is deleted, the identity is automatically removed. User-assigned managed identities have independent lifecycles.

questions.question: Which Azure AD feature allows you to require MFA when a user signs in from an untrusted network location?
options: Conditional Access policies
Azure AD Identity Protection
Azure AD access reviews
Azure AD Privileged Identity Management
questions.explanation: Conditional Access policies evaluate signals such as user location, device state, and application being accessed. You can configure a policy to require MFA when the sign-in originates from a location not marked as trusted.

questions.question: What is the difference between a service principal and a managed identity in Azure?
options: A managed identity is a special type of service principal whose credentials are automatically managed by Azure
A service principal is a special type of managed identity for Azure resources
Managed identities can only be used with Azure VMs, while service principals work with any resource
Service principals are free, while managed identities require an Azure AD Premium license
questions.explanation: A managed identity is essentially a service principal whose credentials (certificates/secrets) are automatically rotated and managed by the Azure platform, eliminating the need for developers to manage credentials.

questions.question: Which built-in Azure RBAC role provides full access to manage all Azure resources but does not allow assignment of roles?
options: Contributor
Owner
Reader
User Access Administrator
questions.explanation: The Contributor role grants full access to manage all resources but cannot assign roles in Azure RBAC. The Owner role includes everything Contributor has plus the ability to assign roles.

questions.question: In Azure AD PIM, what is the difference between an 'eligible' and an 'active' role assignment?
options: Eligible assignments require activation before the role is usable; active assignments are always on
Eligible assignments are permanent; active assignments expire after 24 hours
Active assignments require MFA; eligible assignments do not
There is no difference; the terms are interchangeable
questions.explanation: An eligible assignment means the user can activate the role when needed (just-in-time access), while an active assignment means the role is permanently assigned without requiring activation.

questions.question: At which scope level in Azure does an RBAC role assignment have the broadest effect?
options: Management group
Subscription
Resource group
Individual resource
questions.explanation: RBAC role assignments are inherited downward. Assignments at the management group level are inherited by all subscriptions, resource groups, and resources within that management group, making it the broadest scope.

questions.question: Which Conditional Access grant control blocks sign-ins from devices that are not enrolled in Intune?
options: Require device to be marked as compliant
Require multi-factor authentication
Require hybrid Azure AD joined device
Require approved client app
questions.explanation: The 'Require device to be marked as compliant' grant control ensures only devices enrolled in Intune and meeting compliance policies can access resources. Devices not enrolled will be blocked.

questions.question: What does an Azure AD access review do?
options: Periodically validates whether users still need their assigned roles or group memberships
Scans Azure resources for security vulnerabilities
Reviews firewall rules for misconfigurations
Audits storage accounts for public access
questions.explanation: Azure AD access reviews automate the periodic review of user access rights, ensuring that only the right people retain access to resources, groups, and roles.

questions.question: Which Azure AD feature detects risky sign-in behaviors such as sign-ins from anonymous IP addresses?
options: Azure AD Identity Protection
Azure AD Conditional Access
Azure AD Privileged Identity Management
Azure AD Application Proxy
questions.explanation: Azure AD Identity Protection uses machine learning to detect suspicious sign-in activities, including sign-ins from anonymous IP addresses, atypical travel, and leaked credentials.

questions.question: Which managed identity type can be shared across multiple Azure resources?
options: User-assigned managed identity
System-assigned managed identity
Application managed identity
Group managed identity
questions.explanation: User-assigned managed identities are created as standalone Azure resources and can be associated with multiple Azure resources. System-assigned identities are tied to a single resource.

questions.question: A company wants to ensure that a developer can deploy resources to a specific resource group but cannot modify resources in other resource groups within the same subscription. What should you do?
options: Assign the Contributor role at the resource group scope
Assign the Contributor role at the subscription scope
Assign the Owner role at the resource group scope
Create a custom role with all permissions at the subscription scope
questions.explanation: Assigning the Contributor role at the specific resource group scope limits the developer's permissions to only that resource group. Subscription-level assignments would grant access to all resource groups.

questions.question: You need to create a Conditional Access policy that allows access to a finance application only from compliant devices AND requires MFA. How should you configure the grant controls?
options: Select both controls and set them to 'Require all the selected controls'
Select both controls and set them to 'Require one of the selected controls'
Create two separate Conditional Access policies, one for each control
Use a session control instead of grant controls
questions.explanation: When you need both conditions to be met, configure both grant controls and select 'Require all the selected controls' (AND logic). 'Require one of the selected controls' uses OR logic.

questions.question: An Azure Function app needs to access secrets stored in Azure Key Vault. The security team wants to avoid storing any credentials in code or configuration. What should you use?
options: System-assigned managed identity with a Key Vault access policy
A service principal with a client secret stored in app settings
A shared access signature (SAS) token for Key Vault
Azure AD Application Proxy with certificate authentication
questions.explanation: A system-assigned managed identity eliminates the need to store credentials. You enable it on the Function app and grant it access to Key Vault via an access policy or RBAC role. Azure handles token management automatically.

questions.question: Your organization requires that all Global Administrator role activations in PIM must be approved by another admin and include a justification. Where do you configure this?
options: In the PIM role settings for Global Administrator under the Activation tab
In the Conditional Access policy targeting the PIM application
In the Azure AD security defaults configuration
In the Azure AD tenant properties
questions.explanation: PIM role settings for each role allow you to configure activation requirements including approval workflows, justification requirements, and MFA enforcement on the Activation tab.

questions.question: You need to create a custom RBAC role that allows users to restart VMs but not delete or create them. Which property defines what the role can do?
options: Actions and NotActions in the role definition
AssignableScopes in the role definition
Conditions in the role assignment
DataActions in the role definition
questions.explanation: The Actions property lists allowed management plane operations (like Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/restart/action), and NotActions excludes specific operations. DataActions apply to data plane operations, not management plane.

questions.question: You are configuring Conditional Access. Users report they are prompted for MFA every time they sign in, even from the same trusted device. What should you enable to reduce MFA fatigue?
options: Configure sign-in frequency session control to allow persistent browser sessions
Disable the Conditional Access policy and use Security Defaults instead
Switch from Azure MFA to a third-party MFA solution
Remove the MFA requirement from the grant controls
questions.explanation: Session controls in Conditional Access allow you to set sign-in frequency and persistent browser session settings. This reduces MFA prompts on trusted devices while maintaining security.

questions.question: Which Identity Protection risk policy should you configure to automatically force a password change when a user's credentials are found in a data breach?
options: User risk policy set to require password change
Sign-in risk policy set to require MFA
Conditional Access policy with device compliance
PIM activation policy with approval workflow
questions.explanation: The user risk policy in Identity Protection detects leaked credentials and can be configured to automatically require a secure password change when a user's risk level reaches the configured threshold.

questions.question: A storage team needs read access to blob data in a storage account but should not be able to manage the storage account itself. Which role should you assign?
options: Storage Blob Data Reader
Reader
Storage Account Contributor
Contributor
questions.explanation: Storage Blob Data Reader grants read access to blob data (data plane) without management plane permissions. The Reader role only reads resource metadata, not data. Storage Account Contributor manages the account but not data.

questions.question: You want to block legacy authentication protocols that do not support MFA. Which Conditional Access configuration should you use?
options: Create a policy targeting 'Other clients' under client apps and set the grant to 'Block access'
Create a policy requiring MFA for all client apps
Disable IMAP and POP3 at the Exchange Online level only
Configure Azure AD Identity Protection to block legacy protocols
questions.explanation: Conditional Access can target legacy authentication by selecting 'Other clients' under the client apps condition. Setting the grant control to 'Block access' prevents these protocols from authenticating.

questions.question: An application registered in Azure AD needs to call the Microsoft Graph API to read user profiles without a signed-in user. What type of permission should be configured?
options: Application permissions with admin consent
Delegated permissions with user consent
Delegated permissions with admin consent
Application permissions with user consent
questions.explanation: Application permissions (app roles) are used when an application calls an API without a signed-in user (daemon/service scenario). These always require admin consent because they grant the app direct access.

questions.question: Your security policy requires that members of the Security Administrator role must re-authenticate with MFA every 4 hours. How do you enforce this?
options: Create a Conditional Access policy targeting the role with sign-in frequency set to 4 hours
Configure PIM to require activation every 4 hours
Set the Azure AD session lifetime to 4 hours globally
Enable continuous access evaluation for the role
questions.explanation: Conditional Access sign-in frequency session controls can be targeted at specific directory roles. Setting the frequency to 4 hours forces re-authentication at that interval.

questions.question: You need to allow a vendor's application to access your Azure resources without sharing passwords. The vendor has their own Azure AD tenant. What should you use?
options: Configure a cross-tenant access policy and use federated identity credentials
Create a guest account for each vendor employee in your tenant
Share a client secret with the vendor to use in their application
Create a VPN connection between your network and the vendor's network
questions.explanation: Cross-tenant access policies and federated identity credentials enable workload identity federation, allowing external applications to authenticate without shared secrets by trusting tokens from the vendor's identity provider.

questions.question: You are configuring Azure AD PIM for the Subscription Contributor role. You want eligible users to activate the role for a maximum of 4 hours and require approval from a specific group. You also want to receive notifications when the role is activated. Which three settings must you configure?
options: Maximum activation duration, approval requirement with approvers, and notification settings in PIM role settings
Conditional Access policy, PIM approval workflow, and Azure Monitor alert
Custom RBAC role, PIM eligible assignment, and Log Analytics query
Access review, PIM activation alert, and Azure AD audit log
questions.explanation: All three requirements are configured within PIM role settings: the Activation tab for maximum duration and approval/approvers, and the Notification tab for activation email notifications.

questions.question: A Conditional Access policy requires MFA for all users accessing SharePoint Online. Users report that they are not prompted for MFA when using the SharePoint mobile app. What is the most likely cause?
options: The policy targets the SharePoint Online cloud app but the mobile app uses a different app ID
Mobile devices are excluded from the Conditional Access policy
The SharePoint mobile app does not support modern authentication
MFA is not supported on mobile devices
questions.explanation: Conditional Access policies target specific cloud apps by their app ID. If the mobile app uses a different service principal or app ID than what the policy targets, it may not be evaluated. You should verify the targeted applications include Office 365 SharePoint Online.

questions.question: Your organization uses Azure RBAC with ABAC (attribute-based access control) conditions. You need to restrict a user's Storage Blob Data Contributor role so they can only access blobs with a specific index tag. How do you implement this?
options: Add a role assignment condition that checks blob index tags
Create a custom role with DataActions filtered by tag
Use an Azure Policy to deny access to untagged blobs
Configure a storage account firewall rule based on tags
questions.explanation: Azure ABAC allows you to add conditions to role assignments that evaluate attributes like blob index tags. This provides fine-grained access control beyond what standard RBAC roles offer.

questions.question: An application uses a user-assigned managed identity to access multiple Azure services. You discover that the identity has more permissions than needed. How should you remediate while minimizing disruption?
options: Review the identity's sign-in logs, identify actually used permissions, and create a custom role with only those permissions
Delete the user-assigned managed identity and create a system-assigned identity on each resource
Remove all role assignments and wait for the application team to report issues
Convert the user-assigned managed identity to a service principal with a client secret
questions.explanation: The principle of least privilege requires reviewing actual usage (via sign-in and audit logs), then creating a custom role scoped to only the permissions the application actually uses. This avoids disruption while reducing the attack surface.

questions.question: You need to implement a break-glass account strategy for your Azure AD tenant. Which combination of configurations is correct?
options: Cloud-only accounts excluded from all Conditional Access policies, with PIM monitoring and alerts
Federated accounts with MFA enabled and included in all Conditional Access policies
Guest accounts from a partner tenant with Global Administrator role
Service principal accounts with client certificates and no MFA
questions.explanation: Break-glass (emergency access) accounts should be cloud-only (not federated, to avoid dependency on external IdP), excluded from Conditional Access to ensure access during outages, and monitored via PIM alerts for any usage.

questions.question: A security audit reveals that 15 users have permanent Global Administrator role assignments. Management wants to reduce the attack surface without removing necessary access. What is the best approach?
options: Convert permanent assignments to PIM eligible assignments with time-limited activation and approval requirements
Remove all Global Administrator assignments and use the Contributor role instead
Create 15 separate custom roles with identical permissions to Global Administrator
Enable Security Defaults to automatically limit Global Administrator access
questions.explanation: Converting permanent (active) assignments to PIM eligible assignments ensures admins only activate the role when needed, with time limits and approval workflows. This follows the principle of least privilege for privileged access.

questions.question: You configure an Identity Protection user risk policy to require password change at medium risk. A user's risk is elevated to high. The user successfully changes their password, but the risk remains high. What is the most likely cause?
options: The risk detection that triggered the high risk is an offline detection that requires admin dismissal
The password change did not meet complexity requirements
The user risk policy only applies to medium risk, not high
Azure AD is experiencing replication delays
questions.explanation: Some Identity Protection risk detections are offline detections (like leaked credentials confirmed by Microsoft Threat Intelligence) that are not automatically remediated by password change. An administrator must manually dismiss these risks after investigation.

questions.question: Your organization has a Named Location in Conditional Access defined for the corporate office IP range. Users connecting through the corporate VPN are still being prompted for MFA. What should you investigate first?
options: Whether the VPN's egress IP address is included in the Named Location's IP ranges
Whether the users have Azure AD Premium P2 licenses
Whether MFA server is installed on-premises
Whether the VPN supports split tunneling
questions.explanation: Conditional Access evaluates the source IP of the authentication request. If the VPN routes traffic through a different egress IP than what is defined in the Named Location, the location condition will not match and MFA will be required.

questions.question: An Azure DevOps pipeline needs to deploy resources to Azure. The security team prohibits long-lived secrets. The pipeline runs on a self-hosted agent outside Azure. What authentication approach should you use?
options: Workload identity federation with an Azure AD app registration and federated credential
A managed identity assigned to the self-hosted agent
A service principal with a client secret rotated every 90 days
An Azure AD user account with MFA disabled for automation
questions.explanation: Workload identity federation allows external workloads (like self-hosted Azure DevOps agents) to authenticate using federated credentials without secrets. The external system presents a token from its identity provider that Azure AD trusts.

questions.question: You need to configure Continuous Access Evaluation (CAE) for your organization. Which scenario does CAE address that traditional token lifetime policies cannot?
options: Near real-time revocation of access when a user account is disabled or a critical event occurs
Extending token lifetimes beyond the default one hour
Eliminating the need for refresh tokens entirely
Replacing Conditional Access policies with real-time evaluation
questions.explanation: CAE enables resource providers (like Exchange Online and SharePoint) to subscribe to critical Azure AD events. When a user is disabled or their location changes, access can be revoked near-instantly rather than waiting for token expiry.

questions.question: Which authentication method is considered phishing-resistant and meets the highest level of authentication assurance in Azure AD?
options: FIDO2 security keys
SMS-based MFA
Email one-time passcode
Voice call verification
questions.explanation: FIDO2 security keys provide phishing-resistant authentication because they are bound to the specific site origin, making them immune to man-in-the-middle and phishing attacks. They satisfy the highest authentication strength in Azure AD.

questions.question: You assign a deny assignment in Azure RBAC. What effect does this have?
options: It blocks users from performing specific actions even if a role assignment grants them access
It removes all existing role assignments for the user
It prevents the user from signing in to the Azure portal
It disables the user's Azure AD account
questions.explanation: Deny assignments take precedence over role assignments. Even if a user has a role that grants an action, a deny assignment will block that specific action. Deny assignments are typically created by Azure Blueprints or managed apps.

questions.question: You need to configure authentication strength in Conditional Access to require phishing-resistant MFA for admin roles. Which combination of methods should be included in the custom authentication strength?
options: FIDO2 security key and Windows Hello for Business
Microsoft Authenticator push notification and SMS
Email OTP and voice call
Certificate-based authentication and TOTP only
questions.explanation: Phishing-resistant MFA methods include FIDO2 security keys, Windows Hello for Business, and certificate-based authentication. These are bound to the device/origin, making them resistant to phishing. Push notifications and SMS are not phishing-resistant.

questions.question: A user-assigned managed identity is used by three different Azure resources. You delete the identity. What happens to those resources?
options: The resources lose the ability to authenticate using that identity and API calls will fail
The resources automatically switch to system-assigned managed identities
Azure creates a new user-assigned managed identity with the same name
The resources continue to function using cached tokens indefinitely
questions.explanation: When a user-assigned managed identity is deleted, all resources that reference it lose the ability to obtain tokens through that identity. Existing tokens expire normally, but no new tokens can be acquired, causing authentication failures.

questions.question: What is the primary function of a Network Security Group (NSG) in Azure?
options: Filter network traffic to and from Azure resources using security rules
Encrypt data in transit between Azure virtual networks
Provide DDoS protection for Azure resources
Route traffic between on-premises and Azure networks
questions.explanation: NSGs contain security rules that allow or deny inbound and outbound network traffic to Azure resources. Each rule specifies source/destination, port, and protocol to filter traffic at layer 4.

questions.question: Which Azure service provides a managed, cloud-based firewall that protects your Azure Virtual Network resources?
options: Azure Firewall
Network Security Group
Azure Front Door
Azure Traffic Manager
questions.explanation: Azure Firewall is a managed, stateful firewall-as-a-service with built-in high availability and unrestricted cloud scalability. It provides both network and application-level filtering for traffic flowing through Azure VNets.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a service endpoint and a private endpoint in Azure?
options: A service endpoint routes traffic over the Microsoft backbone but uses public IPs; a private endpoint provides a private IP address within your VNet
A service endpoint encrypts data; a private endpoint does not
A service endpoint is free; a private endpoint requires Premium tier
There is no difference; the terms are interchangeable
questions.explanation: Service endpoints route traffic to Azure PaaS services over the Microsoft backbone network but the service still uses its public IP. Private endpoints assign a private IP from your VNet's address space, making the service accessible only via private networking.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Application Security Groups (ASGs) in Azure?
options: Group VMs logically so you can reference them by name in NSG rules instead of by IP address
Encrypt application traffic between VMs
Monitor application performance metrics
Automatically scale application instances
questions.explanation: ASGs allow you to group VMs by application function (e.g., web servers, database servers) and use those groups as source or destination in NSG rules, eliminating the need to manage individual IP addresses.

questions.question: Which Azure DDoS Protection tier provides adaptive tuning and attack mitigation metrics?
options: DDoS Network Protection (formerly Standard)
DDoS Infrastructure Protection (formerly Basic)
Azure Firewall Premium
Azure Front Door WAF
questions.explanation: DDoS Network Protection provides adaptive tuning based on traffic patterns, real-time attack metrics, diagnostic logging, and cost protection. The Basic/Infrastructure tier provides only default platform-level protection.

questions.question: Which Azure service provides layer 7 load balancing with an integrated Web Application Firewall (WAF)?
options: Azure Application Gateway
Azure Load Balancer
Azure Traffic Manager
Azure Bastion
questions.explanation: Azure Application Gateway is a layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) load balancer that includes an optional WAF component for protecting web applications against common exploits like SQL injection and XSS.

questions.question: What does Azure Bastion provide?
options: Secure RDP and SSH connectivity to VMs directly through the Azure portal without exposing public IPs
A managed VPN gateway for site-to-site connections
DDoS protection for public-facing applications
A content delivery network for static web content
questions.explanation: Azure Bastion is a PaaS service deployed inside your VNet that provides secure RDP/SSH access to VMs via the Azure portal over TLS, eliminating the need for public IP addresses on VMs.

questions.question: Which VPN type provides the most private and reliable connection between on-premises and Azure?
options: ExpressRoute
Site-to-site VPN
Point-to-site VPN
VNet peering
questions.explanation: ExpressRoute provides a private, dedicated connection between your on-premises infrastructure and Azure through a connectivity provider, bypassing the public internet for higher reliability, faster speeds, and lower latency.

questions.question: What happens when you enable a service endpoint for Azure Storage on a subnet?
options: Traffic from that subnet to Azure Storage routes over the Microsoft backbone network
Azure Storage gets a private IP in the subnet
All other subnets lose access to Azure Storage
Data stored in Azure Storage is encrypted with a customer-managed key
questions.explanation: Service endpoints add optimal routes for traffic to Azure PaaS services, routing it directly over the Microsoft backbone network instead of the public internet. The service still uses its public IP address.

questions.question: NSG rules are evaluated in which order?
options: By priority number, lowest number first
By priority number, highest number first
In the order they were created
Alphabetically by rule name
questions.explanation: NSG rules are processed in priority order, starting with the lowest number (highest priority). Once a matching rule is found, processing stops. Rules with lower priority numbers are evaluated before rules with higher numbers.

questions.question: What does Azure Web Application Firewall (WAF) primarily protect against?
options: Common web vulnerabilities such as SQL injection and cross-site scripting
DDoS attacks at the network layer
Brute-force password attacks
Malware distribution through file downloads
questions.explanation: Azure WAF provides centralized protection for web applications against common exploits and vulnerabilities, including OWASP Top 10 threats like SQL injection, XSS, and other HTTP-based attacks.

questions.question: You have a subnet with an NSG applied at the subnet level and another NSG applied to a VM's NIC in that subnet. How are the rules evaluated for inbound traffic?
options: The subnet NSG is evaluated first, then the NIC NSG; traffic must be allowed by both
Only the NIC NSG is evaluated; it overrides the subnet NSG
Only the subnet NSG is evaluated; NIC-level NSGs are ignored
Both NSGs are merged into a single rule set and evaluated together
questions.explanation: For inbound traffic, the subnet-level NSG is evaluated first. If traffic is allowed, it then passes to the NIC-level NSG. Traffic must be allowed by both NSGs to reach the VM. For outbound, the order is reversed (NIC first, then subnet).

questions.question: You need to restrict access to an Azure SQL Database so it is only accessible from a specific VNet subnet. Which approach provides the most secure configuration?
options: Create a private endpoint for the SQL Database in the target subnet
Enable a service endpoint for SQL on the subnet and add a VNet rule to the SQL firewall
Add the subnet's IP range to the SQL Database's IP firewall rules
Deploy the SQL Database inside a VNet using VNet injection
questions.explanation: A private endpoint assigns a private IP from the VNet to the SQL Database, making it accessible only over the private network. This is more secure than service endpoints because no traffic traverses public IP space.

questions.question: You need to allow traffic between two Azure VNets in different regions. Which networking feature should you use?
options: Global VNet peering
Regional VNet peering
Azure ExpressRoute
Azure Traffic Manager
questions.explanation: Global VNet peering connects virtual networks across different Azure regions over the Microsoft backbone network. Regional peering only works within the same region. ExpressRoute connects on-premises to Azure.

questions.question: Your organization deploys Azure Firewall Premium. Which feature does Premium add over Standard that helps detect encrypted malicious traffic?
options: TLS inspection (TLS termination and re-encryption)
Threat intelligence-based filtering
Network address translation (NAT) rules
Application FQDN rules
questions.explanation: Azure Firewall Premium adds TLS inspection, which terminates and inspects encrypted TLS connections for malicious content, then re-encrypts the traffic. The Standard tier cannot inspect encrypted traffic.

questions.question: You configure a private endpoint for an Azure Key Vault. Users can still access the Key Vault via its public FQDN from the internet. What additional step is needed?
options: Disable public network access on the Key Vault's networking settings
Delete the private endpoint and recreate it
Add a DNS A record pointing to the private IP
Enable the Key Vault's built-in firewall
questions.explanation: Creating a private endpoint alone does not disable public access. You must also disable public network access in the Key Vault's networking settings to ensure traffic can only flow through the private endpoint.

questions.question: You need to force all internet-bound traffic from a subnet through an Azure Firewall for inspection. How do you accomplish this?
options: Create a user-defined route (UDR) with 0.0.0.0/0 pointing to the Azure Firewall's private IP as the next hop
Add an NSG rule to the subnet that redirects traffic to Azure Firewall
Enable forced tunneling on the Azure Firewall instance
Configure a NAT rule on Azure Firewall for outbound traffic
questions.explanation: A UDR (route table) with the default route (0.0.0.0/0) pointing to the Azure Firewall's private IP as the next hop forces all internet-bound traffic through the firewall for inspection before leaving the VNet.

questions.question: Which Azure WAF mode allows you to monitor and log potential attacks without blocking any traffic?
options: Detection mode
Prevention mode
Learning mode
Audit mode
questions.explanation: In Detection mode, the WAF monitors and logs all threat detections without blocking any requests. Prevention mode actively blocks requests that match WAF rules. Detection mode is useful for tuning before enabling blocking.

questions.question: You need to connect your on-premises network to Azure with a site-to-site VPN. Which Azure resource must you create to represent your on-premises VPN device?
options: Local network gateway
Virtual network gateway
Application gateway
Azure Bastion
questions.explanation: The local network gateway represents your on-premises VPN device and its configuration (public IP, address space). The virtual network gateway is the Azure-side VPN endpoint. Together they establish the site-to-site VPN tunnel.

questions.question: You need to log all traffic that is allowed or denied by an NSG. Which feature should you enable?
options: NSG flow logs
Azure Firewall logs
Network Watcher packet capture
Azure Monitor activity logs
questions.explanation: NSG flow logs record information about IP traffic flowing through an NSG, including whether it was allowed or denied, source/destination IPs, ports, and protocols. Logs are stored in a storage account and can be analyzed with Traffic Analytics.

questions.question: Your Azure Firewall needs to allow HTTPS traffic to specific FQDNs (like *.microsoft.com) while blocking all other outbound traffic. Which rule type should you use?
options: Application rules with FQDN tags or target FQDNs
Network rules with destination IP addresses
NAT rules with DNAT translation
NSG outbound rules with service tags
questions.explanation: Azure Firewall application rules operate at layer 7 and can filter outbound traffic by FQDN. This allows you to specify *.microsoft.com as an allowed destination. Network rules work at layer 4 and cannot filter by domain name.

questions.question: You are configuring Private DNS Zones for private endpoints. Why is DNS configuration critical for private endpoints to work correctly?
options: Without proper DNS, clients resolve the service's public IP instead of the private endpoint IP
Private endpoints cannot function without a custom DNS server
DNS zones are required to encrypt traffic to the private endpoint
Private DNS zones automatically create the private endpoint
questions.explanation: When a private endpoint is created, the service FQDN must resolve to the private IP. Without proper DNS integration (via Private DNS Zone or custom DNS), clients will resolve the public IP and bypass the private endpoint.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Azure Firewall's threat intelligence-based filtering?
options: Automatically alert on or deny traffic from/to known malicious IP addresses and FQDNs
Filter traffic based on application layer protocols
Provide DDoS protection for the firewall itself
Scan uploaded files for malware
questions.explanation: Azure Firewall's threat intelligence feature uses the Microsoft Threat Intelligence feed to automatically alert on or block traffic from/to known malicious IP addresses, domains, and URLs in real time.

questions.question: Which NSG default rule cannot be deleted and has the lowest priority (highest number)?
options: DenyAllInBound (priority 65500)
AllowVnetInBound (priority 65000)
AllowAzureLoadBalancerInBound (priority 65001)
AllowInternetOutBound (priority 65001)
questions.explanation: The DenyAllInBound rule (priority 65500) is a default rule that denies all inbound traffic not matched by higher-priority rules. Default rules cannot be deleted but can be overridden by creating rules with lower priority numbers.

questions.question: Your organization has a hub-and-spoke network topology with Azure Firewall in the hub VNet. Spoke VNets are peered with the hub. Traffic between spokes does not flow through the firewall. What must you configure to force inter-spoke traffic through the firewall?
options: Disable 'Use remote gateways' on spoke peerings and add UDRs on spoke subnets with the firewall as next hop
Enable VNet peering between all spokes directly
Configure Azure Firewall DNAT rules for each spoke
Enable Azure Firewall's built-in spoke routing feature
questions.explanation: In a hub-and-spoke topology, you need UDRs on each spoke subnet routing inter-spoke traffic (and optionally internet traffic) to the Azure Firewall's private IP. Without UDRs, traffic follows default peering routes that bypass the firewall.

questions.question: You need to segment a large VNet into micro-segments where VMs in the same subnet can be isolated from each other. NSGs alone are insufficient because VMs share the same subnet. What should you use?
options: Application Security Groups (ASGs) with NSG rules to control traffic between logical groups within the same subnet
Multiple subnets with individual NSGs for each VM
Azure Firewall with DNAT rules for intra-subnet traffic
VNet peering between dedicated VNets for each VM
questions.explanation: ASGs allow you to group VMs logically regardless of their subnet and create NSG rules between those groups. This enables micro-segmentation within a single subnet without needing individual IP-based rules.

questions.question: Your organization requires that Azure Firewall inspect all traffic, including traffic to Azure PaaS services like Azure SQL Database. Currently, PaaS traffic bypasses the firewall via service endpoints. How should you redesign this?
options: Replace service endpoints with private endpoints and route traffic through Azure Firewall using UDRs
Add Azure SQL's public IPs to the Azure Firewall's network rules
Enable forced tunneling on the service endpoints
Configure Azure Firewall NAT rules for each PaaS service
questions.explanation: Private endpoints route traffic through the VNet (and therefore through Azure Firewall via UDRs), while service endpoints route traffic directly over the backbone, bypassing the firewall. Switching to private endpoints enables full inspection.

questions.question: You are implementing Azure DDoS Network Protection. Which additional benefit does it provide beyond DDoS Infrastructure Protection?
options: Cost protection with credit for resource costs incurred during a verified DDoS attack
Automatic encryption of all network traffic
Integration with Azure AD for identity-based network policies
Free Azure Firewall Premium for protected VNets
questions.explanation: DDoS Network Protection includes cost protection, meaning Azure provides credits for scale-out costs (like additional compute or bandwidth) incurred during a verified DDoS attack. It also provides attack metrics, alerting, and access to the DDoS Rapid Response team.

questions.question: An ExpressRoute circuit with Microsoft peering is configured. You need to ensure that only your Azure resources are accessible over the circuit and not all Microsoft 365 endpoints. What should you configure?
options: Route filters on the ExpressRoute circuit to advertise only Azure public service prefixes
An NSG on the ExpressRoute gateway subnet
Azure Firewall rules to block Microsoft 365 traffic
A separate ExpressRoute circuit for Microsoft 365
questions.explanation: Route filters allow you to selectively consume Azure services through ExpressRoute Microsoft peering. By configuring route filters, you control which Azure service prefixes are advertised to your on-premises network.

questions.question: You configure Azure Firewall TLS inspection to inspect HTTPS traffic. Internal applications start failing with certificate errors. What is the most likely cause and fix?
options: The enterprise CA certificate used by the firewall is not trusted by the clients; deploy the CA certificate to all client trust stores
The Azure Firewall does not support TLS 1.2; upgrade to TLS 1.3
TLS inspection is incompatible with internal applications; disable it for internal traffic
The applications use certificate pinning and you need to add them to the bypass list
questions.explanation: While deploying the CA certificate to clients (option A) fixes most issues, applications using certificate pinning will still fail because they expect a specific certificate. The correct approach is to add pinned applications to the TLS inspection bypass list.

questions.question: You need to secure an Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) cluster so that all egress traffic is inspected. AKS requires certain FQDNs to function. What must you configure?
options: Azure Firewall with application rules for required AKS FQDNs and a UDR on the AKS subnet routing 0.0.0.0/0 to the firewall
An NSG on the AKS subnet blocking all outbound traffic except required IPs
A private endpoint for each external service AKS needs to access
Azure Front Door with WAF rules for AKS traffic
questions.explanation: To inspect AKS egress traffic, route it through Azure Firewall using a UDR and configure application rules to allow required FQDNs (MCR, management API, etc.). Without the required FQDN allowances, AKS operations will fail.

questions.question: Your Azure Firewall logs show that legitimate traffic to api.contoso.com is being blocked by a network rule. You have an application rule allowing *.contoso.com. What is the issue?
options: Network rules are processed before application rules; a deny network rule is blocking the traffic before the application rule is evaluated
Application rules do not support wildcard FQDNs
The firewall requires a restart to apply new application rules
Azure Firewall cannot filter traffic by FQDN on non-standard ports
questions.explanation: Azure Firewall processes rules in this order: network rules first, then application rules. If a network rule explicitly denies traffic matching the destination, the application rule is never evaluated. Ensure network rules do not conflict with application rules.

questions.question: You are designing a multi-region architecture with Azure Front Door and WAF. How should you configure the WAF to protect against bot traffic while minimizing false positives for legitimate API clients?
options: Enable the bot manager rule set in detection mode first, create custom rules to allow known API client IPs, then switch to prevention mode
Block all traffic from non-browser user agents using a custom rule
Enable rate limiting to 1 request per second for all endpoints
Use geo-filtering to block all traffic from outside your country
questions.explanation: Starting in detection mode allows you to identify false positives from legitimate API clients. Adding custom allow rules for known clients, then switching to prevention mode, ensures bot protection without disrupting legitimate traffic.

questions.question: You need to ensure that Azure management traffic (such as Azure Resource Manager API calls) from VMs in a subnet cannot be intercepted. Which combination provides the strongest protection?
options: Use private endpoints for Azure Resource Manager and route traffic through Azure Firewall with TLS inspection disabled for ARM traffic
Enable service endpoints for Azure Resource Manager on the subnet
Configure an NSG to allow only HTTPS outbound traffic
Deploy Azure Bastion and access VMs only through the Azure portal
questions.explanation: Private endpoints for ARM ensure management traffic stays within the private network. Disabling TLS inspection for ARM traffic prevents certificate issues while maintaining privacy. This is the strongest approach for protecting management plane traffic.

questions.question: Which Azure service should you use to analyze NSG flow logs and visualize traffic patterns across your Azure network?
options: Traffic Analytics (part of Network Watcher)
Azure Monitor Metrics
Azure Advisor
Microsoft Defender for Cloud
questions.explanation: Traffic Analytics processes NSG flow logs and provides visualizations of traffic flow patterns, top talkers, security threats, and more. It is a feature of Azure Network Watcher.

questions.question: You need to protect a public-facing web application from OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities. The application is hosted on Azure App Service. Which architecture is recommended?
options: Azure Application Gateway with WAF v2 in front of the App Service
An NSG applied to the App Service
Azure DDoS Network Protection on the App Service plan
Azure Firewall with DNAT rules pointing to the App Service
questions.explanation: Application Gateway with WAF v2 provides layer 7 protection against OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities (SQL injection, XSS, etc.) and is the recommended approach for protecting web applications on App Service.

questions.question: You configure a private endpoint for Azure Blob Storage. Which private DNS zone name should you use for automatic DNS resolution?
options: privatelink.blob.core.windows.net
privatelink.storage.azure.net
blob.core.windows.net
private.blob.core.windows.net
questions.explanation: Azure private endpoints for Blob Storage require a Private DNS Zone named privatelink.blob.core.windows.net. This zone resolves the storage account's FQDN to its private IP address within the VNet.

questions.question: You are auditing NSG flow logs and notice that VMs in a subnet are sending DNS queries to an external DNS server instead of Azure DNS. This violates your security policy. How should you block this?
options: Add an NSG rule to deny outbound UDP and TCP port 53 to all destinations except 168.63.129.16 (Azure DNS)
Disable DNS resolution on the VNet
Configure Azure Firewall DNS proxy and block all direct DNS traffic
Remove the external DNS server from the VNet's DNS settings
questions.explanation: Adding an NSG rule to deny outbound DNS traffic (port 53) except to Azure DNS (168.63.129.16) ensures VMs can only use Azure-provided DNS. This prevents DNS exfiltration or policy violations.

questions.question: What is Azure Key Vault primarily used for?
options: Centrally managing secrets, encryption keys, and certificates
Storing large files and binary data
Managing virtual machine configurations
Monitoring application performance
questions.explanation: Azure Key Vault provides a centralized, secure store for managing secrets (passwords, connection strings), encryption keys, and certificates. It integrates with Azure services and supports hardware security modules (HSMs).

questions.question: What is Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) in Azure SQL Database?
options: Encryption of the database, backups, and transaction logs at rest without application changes
Encryption of data in transit between the application and the database
Column-level encryption that requires application changes
Encryption of the virtual machine disk where SQL Server is installed
questions.explanation: TDE encrypts the storage of an entire database at rest (data files, log files, and backups) using a symmetric key called the database encryption key. It requires no application changes and is enabled by default on Azure SQL Database.

questions.question: Which Azure service provides security recommendations and a Secure Score for your Azure resources?
options: Microsoft Defender for Cloud
Azure Monitor
Azure Advisor
Azure Policy
questions.explanation: Microsoft Defender for Cloud provides a unified security management system that includes a Secure Score (measuring your security posture), security recommendations, and advanced threat protection across Azure, hybrid, and multi-cloud resources.

questions.question: What does Azure Disk Encryption use to encrypt VM OS and data disks?
options: BitLocker for Windows VMs and DM-Crypt for Linux VMs
Azure Key Vault for all VM types
TDE for both Windows and Linux VMs
SSL/TLS certificates stored in the VM
questions.explanation: Azure Disk Encryption uses BitLocker (Windows) and DM-Crypt (Linux) to provide volume encryption for the OS and data disks. Encryption keys are stored in Azure Key Vault.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a storage account firewall in Azure?
options: Restrict access to the storage account from specific IP addresses, VNets, or Azure services
Encrypt data stored in the storage account
Monitor storage account access patterns
Replicate data across multiple regions
questions.explanation: Storage account firewalls restrict network access by allowing traffic only from specified IP addresses, VNets (via service endpoints or private endpoints), or trusted Azure services. By default, storage accounts accept traffic from all networks.

questions.question: What is Always Encrypted in Azure SQL Database designed to protect?
options: Sensitive data so that it is never revealed in plaintext to the database engine
The database connection string from being intercepted
The database server from unauthorized network access
Database backups from being restored on unauthorized servers
questions.explanation: Always Encrypted ensures that sensitive data (like credit card numbers or SSNs) is encrypted in the client application and never decrypted inside the database engine. Encryption and decryption happen only in the client driver.

questions.question: Which Azure Key Vault tier uses FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated hardware security modules?
options: Premium tier
Standard tier
Basic tier
Enterprise tier
questions.explanation: Azure Key Vault Premium tier uses FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated HSMs to protect keys. The Standard tier uses software-protected keys (FIPS 140-2 Level 1). There is no Basic or Enterprise tier.

questions.question: By default, all data in Azure Storage is encrypted at rest using which encryption method?
options: Microsoft-managed keys with AES-256 encryption
Customer-managed keys stored in Azure Key Vault
No encryption; it must be manually enabled
SSL/TLS encryption
questions.explanation: Azure Storage automatically encrypts all data at rest using AES-256 encryption with Microsoft-managed keys. This is enabled by default for all storage accounts and cannot be disabled.

questions.question: Which type of shared access signature (SAS) provides the most granular control over Azure Storage access?
options: User delegation SAS (backed by Azure AD credentials)
Account SAS
Service SAS
Container-level SAS
questions.explanation: User delegation SAS is the most secure type because it is signed with Azure AD credentials rather than the storage account key. It provides granular control and the token can be revoked by revoking the user delegation key.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of Microsoft Defender for Servers?
options: Provide threat detection, vulnerability assessment, and security recommendations for server workloads
Back up server data to Azure Blob Storage
Monitor server CPU and memory utilization
Automate server patching and updates
questions.explanation: Microsoft Defender for Servers extends Defender for Cloud to provide threat detection, vulnerability assessment, adaptive application controls, and just-in-time VM access for Azure, on-premises, and multi-cloud server workloads.

questions.question: Which feature of Microsoft Defender for Cloud reduces the attack surface by allowing only approved applications to run on VMs?
options: Adaptive application controls
Just-in-time VM access
File integrity monitoring
Network segmentation recommendations
questions.explanation: Adaptive application controls use machine learning to analyze running processes and create application allowlisting policies. This blocks unauthorized applications from running on protected VMs.

questions.question: You need to encrypt Azure SQL Database with a key that your organization controls and can rotate on its own schedule. What should you configure?
options: TDE with a customer-managed key (CMK) stored in Azure Key Vault
TDE with a service-managed key
Always Encrypted with a column master key
Azure Disk Encryption on the SQL Server VM
questions.explanation: TDE with customer-managed keys (Bring Your Own Key) allows your organization to store the TDE protector key in Azure Key Vault, where you control rotation, permissions, and can revoke access. This meets regulatory requirements for key ownership.

questions.question: You want to be alerted when someone accesses an Azure Key Vault secret outside of business hours. What should you configure?
options: Enable Key Vault diagnostic logging and create an Azure Monitor alert rule based on log queries
Enable Key Vault soft delete
Configure Key Vault purge protection
Enable Key Vault private endpoints
questions.explanation: Enabling diagnostic logs sends Key Vault access events to a Log Analytics workspace. You can then create an alert rule using a KQL query that filters for secret access events outside business hours.

questions.question: A compliance requirement mandates that even Azure administrators cannot read sensitive columns in a SQL database. Which feature should you implement?
options: Always Encrypted with role separation (DBA cannot access column master key)
Transparent Data Encryption with customer-managed keys
Dynamic Data Masking
Row-Level Security
questions.explanation: Always Encrypted with role separation ensures that DBAs who manage the database never see the plaintext data. The column master key is stored in a Key Vault or certificate store that only the application has access to, not the DBA.

questions.question: You need to restrict an Azure Storage account so that only resources from a specific VNet subnet can access it. What is the recommended configuration?
options: Set the storage firewall default action to Deny and add the subnet as an allowed virtual network
Set the storage firewall default action to Allow and add a deny rule for all other subnets
Enable storage encryption with customer-managed keys
Create an Azure Policy that denies storage access from non-approved subnets
questions.explanation: Setting the default action to Deny blocks all traffic except from explicitly allowed networks. Adding the specific VNet/subnet (via service endpoint or private endpoint) creates a network rule that permits access from that source only.

questions.question: Your organization wants to use Azure Key Vault but needs the ability to recover accidentally deleted secrets for up to 90 days. Which feature should you enable?
options: Soft delete (with a retention period of up to 90 days)
Purge protection
Key Vault backup and restore
Key Vault replication
questions.explanation: Soft delete allows recovery of deleted Key Vault objects (secrets, keys, certificates) within the retention period (7-90 days). Once enabled, deleted objects are retained in a recoverable state instead of being permanently removed.

questions.question: You need to implement just-in-time (JIT) VM access. What does JIT VM access do when approved?
options: Temporarily opens specific ports on the NSG for a limited time to the requester's IP
Creates a temporary VPN connection to the VM
Starts the VM and stops it after the access window expires
Provides temporary Azure AD credentials for the VM
questions.explanation: JIT VM access modifies NSG rules to temporarily allow inbound traffic to specified management ports (like RDP/SSH) from the requester's IP address for a limited duration. When the window expires, the rules are automatically reverted.

questions.question: You are securing an Azure Container Registry (ACR). Which feature helps ensure that only signed and verified images can be deployed?
options: Content trust (Docker Content Trust / Notary)
ACR replication
ACR build tasks
ACR webhooks
questions.explanation: Content trust in ACR uses Docker Content Trust (based on Notary) to sign container images. When enabled, only images with valid signatures can be pulled, preventing deployment of tampered or unauthorized images.

questions.question: Your Secure Score in Microsoft Defender for Cloud is 45%. Which action would typically improve the score the most?
options: Remediate the recommendations with the highest point value first
Enable all Defender plans regardless of cost
Add more resources to the subscription
Disable security recommendations that are hard to implement
questions.explanation: Secure Score is calculated based on the ratio of healthy resources to total resources for each recommendation. Remediating recommendations with the highest point values has the biggest impact on improving the overall score.

questions.question: You need to ensure that a deleted Azure Key Vault cannot be permanently purged until the retention period expires, even by an administrator. What should you enable?
options: Purge protection
Soft delete
Key Vault RBAC
Key Vault private endpoint
questions.explanation: Purge protection prevents permanent deletion (purging) of a soft-deleted Key Vault or its objects before the retention period expires. Even Global Administrators cannot override this, providing protection against insider threats.

questions.question: You need to mask credit card numbers displayed to helpdesk users querying an Azure SQL Database, but finance users should see the full numbers. Which feature should you use?
options: Dynamic Data Masking with rules that exclude the finance users' role
Always Encrypted with role separation
Row-Level Security filtering by user role
Transparent Data Encryption with separate keys
questions.explanation: Dynamic Data Masking obfuscates sensitive data in query results for designated users while leaving it visible to authorized users. You can exclude specific users or roles from masking. It does not encrypt the data at rest.

questions.question: Which Key Vault access model is recommended by Microsoft for new deployments?
options: Azure RBAC (role-based access control) for the data plane
Vault access policies
Shared access signatures
Managed identity with access keys
questions.explanation: Microsoft recommends Azure RBAC for Key Vault data plane access in new deployments. It provides a unified authorization model across Azure, supports granular permissions via built-in roles, and integrates with Conditional Access.

questions.question: You need to detect and alert on SQL injection attempts against your Azure SQL Database. Which Defender plan should you enable?
options: Microsoft Defender for SQL
Microsoft Defender for Servers
Microsoft Defender for Storage
Microsoft Defender for Key Vault
questions.explanation: Microsoft Defender for SQL provides Advanced Threat Protection, which detects anomalous activities including SQL injection attempts, unusual access patterns, and brute-force attacks against Azure SQL databases.

questions.question: Your organization requires double encryption for data at rest in Azure Storage. You already have Microsoft-managed encryption. How do you implement the second layer?
options: Enable infrastructure encryption on the storage account, which adds a second layer of AES-256 with a separate Microsoft-managed key
Enable customer-managed keys, which replaces the first layer with a second one
Enable Azure Disk Encryption on storage accounts
Configure two separate Key Vault keys and assign both to the storage account
questions.explanation: Infrastructure encryption adds a second layer of AES-256 encryption at the storage infrastructure level using a separate Microsoft-managed key. Combined with the default encryption layer, this provides double encryption for compliance requirements.

questions.question: You are implementing Azure Confidential Computing for a workload that processes highly sensitive data. Which feature ensures data is protected while being processed in memory?
options: Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) using hardware-based enclaves like Intel SGX or AMD SEV-SNP
Azure Disk Encryption for the VM's OS disk
Always Encrypted for the database layer
Azure Key Vault Premium with HSM-backed keys
questions.explanation: Azure Confidential Computing uses hardware-based Trusted Execution Environments (Intel SGX enclaves or AMD SEV-SNP) to protect data in use — while it is being processed in memory. This protects against even privileged access to the host.

questions.question: Your Key Vault is using vault access policies. An application has Get and List permissions for secrets. A developer also has Get and List permissions. You discover the developer can read production secrets they should not have access to. What is the best remediation?
options: Migrate to Azure RBAC for Key Vault and assign granular roles scoped to specific secrets using conditions
Remove the developer's access policy entirely
Create a separate Key Vault for each environment
Enable Key Vault firewall to block the developer's IP
questions.explanation: Vault access policies apply to all objects of a type — you cannot scope them to individual secrets. Migrating to Azure RBAC allows granular role assignments with conditions, enabling you to restrict access to specific secrets while maintaining other access.

questions.question: A security scan reveals that several Azure Storage accounts have public blob access enabled. You need to ensure that no storage account in the subscription can ever have public blob access. What is the most scalable approach?
options: Create an Azure Policy that denies storage accounts with public blob access enabled
Manually disable public access on each storage account
Create an NSG rule to block public traffic to storage accounts
Enable Defender for Storage to block public access
questions.explanation: Azure Policy can enforce organizational standards at scale. A deny policy for public blob access ensures no existing or future storage account can enable public access, providing automated, continuous compliance enforcement.

questions.question: You configured Always Encrypted on a SQL Database column. Users querying the column through SSMS see encrypted values. An application team reports their .NET application also shows encrypted values. What is the most likely issue?
options: The application's connection string is missing 'Column Encryption Setting=Enabled'
The column master key has expired
The database does not have TDE enabled
The application needs to use a different SQL driver
questions.explanation: Always Encrypted requires the client application to include 'Column Encryption Setting=Enabled' in the connection string. Without it, the ADO.NET driver does not perform the encryption/decryption operations and returns the raw encrypted values.

questions.question: Microsoft Defender for Cloud shows that your Secure Score has decreased after adding new resources. No existing configurations changed. Why did the score decrease?
options: New resources introduced unhealthy recommendations, increasing the total recommendations denominator
Microsoft updated the scoring algorithm to be stricter
Existing resources became non-compliant due to new Azure Policy updates
The Defender plans expired and need renewal
questions.explanation: Secure Score = (healthy resources) / (total resources) per recommendation. Adding new resources that have unresolved recommendations increases the denominator, lowering the overall percentage even if existing resources remain healthy.

questions.question: You need to implement customer-managed keys (CMK) for Azure Disk Encryption. The key must be stored in a dedicated, single-tenant HSM that you fully control. Which Azure service should you use?
options: Azure Dedicated HSM or Azure Key Vault Managed HSM
Azure Key Vault Standard tier
Azure Key Vault Premium tier
Azure Storage Service Encryption with CMK
questions.explanation: Azure Dedicated HSM provides single-tenant FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated HSMs that you fully control. Azure Key Vault Managed HSM also provides single-tenant HSMs. Both options meet the requirement for dedicated, customer-controlled key storage.

questions.question: Your organization uses Defender for Cloud's regulatory compliance dashboard. An audit requires proof that all Azure SQL databases enforce a minimum TLS version of 1.2. How do you demonstrate this?
options: Export the compliance assessment report for the relevant regulatory standard showing the TLS recommendation status
Run a SQL query against each database to check the TLS version
Provide screenshots of each database's networking settings
Show the Azure Policy compliance report for a different policy
questions.explanation: Defender for Cloud's regulatory compliance dashboard maps security recommendations to regulatory controls. You can export the assessment showing the status of the 'minimum TLS version' recommendation for all SQL databases as audit evidence.

questions.question: An Azure Key Vault's diagnostic logs show a spike in 403 (Forbidden) errors from an application's managed identity. The application was working yesterday. What should you investigate first?
options: Whether the Key Vault access policy or RBAC assignment for the managed identity was modified or removed
Whether the Key Vault's encryption key has expired
Whether the managed identity's password has expired
Whether Azure Key Vault is experiencing a regional outage
questions.explanation: 403 errors indicate an authorization failure. The most common cause is a change to access policies or RBAC assignments. Managed identities do not have passwords. Check the Key Vault audit log for recent permission changes.

questions.question: You need to ensure that Azure Container Instances (ACI) running in your environment cannot access the Azure Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) endpoint. Why is this important and how do you mitigate it?
options: IMDS can expose managed identity tokens; deploy ACI in a VNet with an NSG blocking 169.254.169.254
IMDS provides container logs; this is not a security concern
IMDS only works with VMs, not containers, so no action is needed
Configure ACI to use a service principal instead of managed identity
questions.explanation: The IMDS endpoint (169.254.169.254) can be accessed by containers to retrieve managed identity tokens. If a container is compromised, the attacker could use these tokens. Blocking IMDS access via network controls limits this attack vector.

questions.question: Which encryption option allows you to search or perform equality comparisons on encrypted columns in Azure SQL Database?
options: Always Encrypted with deterministic encryption
Always Encrypted with randomized encryption
Transparent Data Encryption
Dynamic Data Masking
questions.explanation: Deterministic encryption always produces the same encrypted value for a given plaintext value, enabling equality comparisons, joins, and grouping. Randomized encryption provides higher security but does not support any operations on encrypted data.

questions.question: You need to audit all read operations on an Azure Storage account's blobs for compliance purposes. What should you enable?
options: Storage Analytics logging or Azure Monitor diagnostic settings for blob read operations
Azure Activity Log for the storage account
NSG flow logs for the storage account's VNet
Defender for Storage alerting
questions.explanation: Storage Analytics logging or Azure Monitor diagnostic settings can capture all data plane operations (read, write, delete) on blobs. Activity logs only capture management plane operations, not data access.

questions.question: You are configuring Azure Disk Encryption for a Windows VM. Where must the encryption key be stored?
options: Azure Key Vault (in the same region as the VM)
Azure Storage account
The VM's local disk
Azure AD
questions.explanation: Azure Disk Encryption stores the BitLocker encryption keys as secrets in Azure Key Vault. The Key Vault must be in the same region and subscription as the VM for the encryption to work.

questions.question: You discover that a storage account's shared access key has been compromised. How should you respond to immediately mitigate the risk?
options: Regenerate both access keys for the storage account and update all applications using the keys
Delete the storage account and recreate it
Enable a storage firewall to block all traffic
Revoke all SAS tokens by deleting the stored access policy
questions.explanation: Regenerating both access keys immediately invalidates all SAS tokens signed with those keys. You must then update all applications with the new keys. Simply revoking stored access policies would not invalidate ad-hoc SAS tokens signed with the compromised key.

questions.question: What is Microsoft Sentinel?
options: A cloud-native SIEM and SOAR solution that provides intelligent security analytics and threat intelligence
A virtual machine backup solution
A network monitoring tool for Azure VNets
A compliance management platform
questions.explanation: Microsoft Sentinel is a scalable, cloud-native SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) and SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response) solution that uses AI to analyze large volumes of security data.

questions.question: What query language is used to analyze data in Microsoft Sentinel?
options: Kusto Query Language (KQL)
Structured Query Language (SQL)
PowerShell scripting language
Python
questions.explanation: KQL (Kusto Query Language) is the query language used across Microsoft Sentinel, Log Analytics, and other Azure monitoring services to analyze and visualize log data.

questions.question: What is a playbook in Microsoft Sentinel?
options: An automated workflow built on Azure Logic Apps that responds to security incidents
A document describing incident response procedures
A KQL query saved for repeated use
A dashboard showing security metrics
questions.explanation: Sentinel playbooks are automated response workflows powered by Azure Logic Apps. They can be triggered by analytics rules or incidents to perform actions like sending notifications, blocking IPs, or isolating machines.

questions.question: What is the purpose of data connectors in Microsoft Sentinel?
options: Ingest security data from various sources into the Sentinel workspace
Export Sentinel data to external systems
Connect Sentinel to Azure DevOps pipelines
Create network connections between Azure VNets
questions.explanation: Data connectors are the integration points that bring security data from various sources (Azure services, Microsoft 365, third-party solutions, syslog, CEF) into the Sentinel Log Analytics workspace for analysis.

questions.question: What does Azure Secure Score measure?
options: Your organization's security posture based on how many security recommendations have been implemented
The number of active security incidents in your environment
The amount of data stored in your Azure subscription
The performance of your Azure virtual machines
questions.explanation: Secure Score in Microsoft Defender for Cloud is a percentage that represents how well you have implemented security recommendations. A higher score indicates a stronger security posture.

questions.question: What is the purpose of threat intelligence indicators in Microsoft Sentinel?
options: Provide known indicators of compromise (IoCs) like malicious IPs, domains, and file hashes to detect threats
Predict future security threats using AI
Monitor the threat intelligence of competing organizations
Create new vulnerability signatures for Azure Firewall
questions.explanation: Threat intelligence indicators (IoCs) in Sentinel include known malicious IP addresses, domains, URLs, and file hashes. They are used to correlate with log data to detect known threats in your environment.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Sentinel feature allows you to define detection logic that creates alerts when specific patterns are found in log data?
options: Analytics rules
Workbooks
Watchlists
Repositories
questions.explanation: Analytics rules define the KQL queries and conditions that, when matched, generate security alerts and optionally group them into incidents. They are the core detection mechanism in Microsoft Sentinel.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Incidents page in Microsoft Sentinel?
options: Provide a centralized view for triaging, investigating, and managing security incidents
Display a list of all data connectors
Show the configuration of analytics rules
Manage user access to the Sentinel workspace
questions.explanation: The Incidents page is where security analysts triage, investigate, and manage security incidents. It shows incident severity, status, assigned owner, and provides tools for investigation and response.

questions.question: Which tool in Microsoft Sentinel provides interactive data visualization dashboards?
options: Workbooks
Playbooks
Watchlists
Notebooks
questions.explanation: Workbooks in Sentinel provide interactive dashboards that combine text, KQL queries, metrics, and parameters into rich visual reports. They are used for monitoring, analysis, and creating custom views of security data.

questions.question: What type of log data does the Azure Activity Log capture?
options: Subscription-level events such as resource creation, modification, and deletion (management plane operations)
Application-level performance metrics and traces
Network traffic flow data between Azure resources
User sign-in events and authentication data
questions.explanation: The Azure Activity Log records management plane operations at the subscription level, including who did what, when, and on which resource. Examples include creating a VM, modifying an NSG, or deleting a storage account.

questions.question: What is the primary function of Microsoft Defender for Cloud's security alerts?
options: Notify you when a potential security threat is detected across your Azure resources
Provide billing alerts when costs exceed a threshold
Alert on VM performance degradation
Notify when Azure region capacity is running low
questions.explanation: Security alerts in Defender for Cloud are generated when threats are detected by Defender plans. They provide details about the threat, affected resources, and recommended remediation steps.

questions.question: You need to detect when a user signs in from a country where your organization has no employees. Which Sentinel analytics rule type should you use?
options: Scheduled analytics rule with a KQL query that joins sign-in logs with a watchlist of approved countries
Microsoft Security analytics rule linked to Defender for Cloud
Built-in anomaly detection rule
Near-real-time (NRT) rule triggered by Activity Log events
questions.explanation: A scheduled analytics rule lets you write a custom KQL query that joins Azure AD sign-in logs with a watchlist of approved countries. If a sign-in occurs from a country not in the watchlist, an alert is generated.

questions.question: You want Microsoft Sentinel to automatically create an incident and assign it to the SOC team when a specific analytics rule triggers. What should you configure?
options: Enable incident creation in the analytics rule settings and configure an automation rule to assign the owner
Create a playbook that runs every hour and checks for new alerts
Configure an Azure Monitor action group to email the SOC team
Enable the Microsoft Security analytics rule template
questions.explanation: Analytics rules can be configured to automatically create incidents from alerts. Automation rules in Sentinel can then automatically set the incident owner, severity, or trigger playbooks when incidents are created.

questions.question: You are writing a KQL query to find all failed sign-in attempts in the last 24 hours. Which table and filter should you use in Sentinel?
options: SigninLogs | where TimeGenerated > ago(24h) and ResultType != 0
SecurityEvent | where TimeGenerated > ago(24h) and EventID == 4625
AzureActivity | where TimeGenerated > ago(24h) and ActivityStatus == 'Failed'
AuditLogs | where TimeGenerated > ago(24h) and Result == 'failure'
questions.explanation: The SigninLogs table contains Azure AD sign-in data. ResultType != 0 filters for non-successful (failed) sign-ins. SecurityEvent table 4625 would capture on-premises Windows failed logons, not Azure AD sign-ins.

questions.question: You need to ingest on-premises Windows Security Event logs into Microsoft Sentinel. Which data connector should you use?
options: Windows Security Events via AMA (Azure Monitor Agent)
Azure Activity Log connector
Microsoft 365 Defender connector
Syslog connector
questions.explanation: The Windows Security Events via AMA connector uses the Azure Monitor Agent installed on the Windows servers to collect Security Event logs and forward them to the Sentinel workspace.

questions.question: A Sentinel analytics rule generates too many false positive alerts for a specific source IP that belongs to a vulnerability scanner. How should you reduce noise without disabling the rule?
options: Add the scanner's IP to a watchlist and modify the KQL query to exclude IPs in the watchlist
Delete the analytics rule and create a new one
Increase the alert threshold to 1000 events
Disable the data connector that ingests the firewall logs
questions.explanation: Adding known-good IPs to a watchlist and modifying the query to exclude them is the cleanest approach. This preserves the detection for actual threats while eliminating noise from known authorized scanners.

questions.question: You need to correlate alerts from Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, Defender for Office 365, and Defender for Identity in a single view. Which Microsoft service provides this unified correlation?
options: Microsoft 365 Defender (with incidents that correlate alerts across products)
Microsoft Sentinel only
Microsoft Defender for Cloud only
Azure Monitor
questions.explanation: Microsoft 365 Defender automatically correlates alerts from Defender for Endpoint, Office 365, Identity, and Cloud Apps into unified incidents. These incidents can also be streamed to Microsoft Sentinel for further analysis.

questions.question: You need to investigate a security incident in Sentinel and understand the relationships between entities like users, IPs, and hosts involved. Which feature should you use?
options: Investigation graph
Workbooks
Analytics rules
Data connectors
questions.explanation: The Investigation graph in Sentinel provides a visual, interactive tool for exploring the relationships between entities (users, IPs, hosts, files) connected to a security incident, helping analysts understand the scope and impact of an attack.

questions.question: You want to automate the response when a Sentinel incident is created with high severity. The automation should block the attacker's IP on Azure Firewall. What should you use?
options: An automation rule that triggers a playbook (Logic App) to add the IP to a deny list on Azure Firewall
A KQL query that runs every minute to check for new incidents
An Azure Monitor alert that triggers an Azure Function
An Azure Policy that automatically blocks IPs
