questions.explanation: Automation rules in Sentinel can trigger playbooks (Logic Apps) when incidents are created. The playbook can extract the attacker's IP from the incident entities and use the Azure Firewall REST API to add it to a deny rule.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA) in Microsoft Sentinel?
options: Detect anomalous behaviors by building behavioral profiles for users and entities and identifying deviations
Manage entity relationship diagrams for your infrastructure
Encrypt entity data in transit
Track entity lifecycle from creation to deletion
questions.explanation: UEBA (User and Entity Behavior Analytics) in Sentinel uses machine learning to build behavioral baselines for users, hosts, and IP addresses. It identifies anomalies when entity behavior deviates from the baseline, detecting insider threats and compromised accounts.

questions.question: Which Sentinel feature allows you to upload a CSV file of known IoCs or approved entities for use in KQL queries?
options: Watchlists
Threat Intelligence indicators
Content Hub
Repositories
questions.explanation: Watchlists allow you to upload data (CSV files) like lists of approved IPs, high-value assets, or terminated employees. This data can then be referenced in KQL queries for enrichment, correlation, and exception handling.

questions.question: A security analyst needs to quickly identify the most common alert types generated in the last 7 days in Sentinel. Which KQL query pattern would accomplish this?
options: SecurityAlert | where TimeGenerated > ago(7d) | summarize count() by AlertName | sort by count_ desc
SecurityIncident | where TimeGenerated > ago(7d) | project IncidentName
SigninLogs | where TimeGenerated > ago(7d) | summarize count() by UserPrincipalName
Heartbeat | where TimeGenerated > ago(7d) | count
questions.explanation: This KQL query uses the SecurityAlert table, filters for the last 7 days, summarizes the count by AlertName, and sorts by count descending to show the most frequent alert types first.

questions.question: You need to ensure that Sentinel analytics rules, playbooks, and workbooks are version-controlled and deployed consistently across development and production workspaces. What should you use?
options: Sentinel Repositories (integration with GitHub or Azure DevOps for CI/CD)
Azure Resource Manager templates only
Manual export and import of JSON files
Azure Backup for the Log Analytics workspace
questions.explanation: Sentinel Repositories integrate with GitHub or Azure DevOps to enable version control and CI/CD for Sentinel content (analytics rules, playbooks, workbooks). Changes in the repository are automatically deployed to the workspace.

questions.question: What is the difference between a Log Analytics workspace and a Microsoft Sentinel workspace?
options: Sentinel is a solution that runs on top of a Log Analytics workspace; they share the same underlying data store
They are completely separate services with different data stores
Log Analytics is deprecated and replaced by Sentinel
Sentinel requires a dedicated workspace that cannot be used by other services
questions.explanation: Microsoft Sentinel is enabled on top of a Log Analytics workspace. The workspace is the data store where all logs are collected. Sentinel adds security-specific features (analytics rules, incidents, playbooks) on top of Log Analytics capabilities.

questions.question: You are investigating a potential lateral movement attack in Sentinel. The attacker compromised a user account and may have accessed multiple systems. Which investigation approach provides the most comprehensive view?
options: Use the investigation graph to map the compromised user entity to related IPs, hosts, and accounts, then pivot to hunting queries for each related entity
Search the SecurityAlert table for the user's name
Check the Azure Activity Log for the user's management operations
Review the user's most recent sign-in event only
questions.explanation: The investigation graph maps entity relationships visually. Starting from the compromised user, you can discover related entities (IPs they authenticated from, hosts they accessed, other accounts involved), then run hunting queries to trace lateral movement across the environment.

questions.question: You need to detect a slow brute-force attack where an attacker attempts 5 failed logins per hour for 8 hours against the same account. A standard failed login rule triggers at 10 failures in 5 minutes. How should you design the detection?
options: Create a scheduled rule that runs hourly, uses bin(TimeGenerated, 1h) to bucket events, and triggers when the same account has failures across 8 or more hourly buckets in 24 hours
Lower the standard rule threshold to 2 failures in 60 minutes
Enable the built-in brute-force detection template without modification
Use a NRT (near-real-time) rule to detect each individual failed login
questions.explanation: A slow brute-force attack spreads attempts over time to avoid standard thresholds. A custom scheduled rule that bins events into hourly buckets and checks for persistent failures across multiple hours catches this pattern that a short-window rule would miss.

questions.question: Your Sentinel workspace processes 500 GB of data daily. You need to reduce costs while maintaining detection capabilities for security-critical data. Which data management strategy should you implement?
options: Use basic logs for high-volume, low-security-value tables and analytics logs for security-critical tables; archive older data to long-term retention
Reduce the Log Analytics workspace retention to 7 days
Disable all data connectors and rely solely on Microsoft 365 Defender alerts
Move the Sentinel workspace to a cheaper Azure region
questions.explanation: Basic logs cost significantly less than analytics logs but have limited query capabilities. For high-volume tables with low security value (like verbose debug logs), basic logs reduce costs while analytics logs maintain full capabilities for security-critical data.

questions.question: An analyst notices that Sentinel is creating duplicate incidents for the same attack because multiple analytics rules detect different aspects of it. How should you consolidate these?
options: Configure alert grouping in the analytics rules to group related alerts into a single incident based on entity mapping
Delete all but one analytics rule
Merge incidents manually after they are created
Disable incident creation and only use alerts
questions.explanation: Analytics rules can be configured to group alerts into existing incidents based on shared entities (same user, IP, or host). This prevents duplicate incidents while preserving all detection logic and alert details.

questions.question: You need to detect when an attacker uses a compromised Azure AD service principal to enumerate all users in the directory. Which Sentinel detection approach is most effective?
options: Monitor the AuditLogs table for Microsoft Graph API calls to /users with an unusual service principal, combined with anomalous access patterns from UEBA
Check the SigninLogs table for failed user sign-ins
Monitor the AzureActivity table for subscription-level operations
Enable Defender for Identity to detect this Azure AD-specific threat
questions.explanation: Service principal activity against Microsoft Graph (user enumeration) appears in AuditLogs. Combining this with UEBA to detect anomalous service principal behavior creates an effective detection for this attack pattern.

questions.question: You are building a multi-tenant Sentinel solution using Azure Lighthouse. What is the primary security concern with this architecture?
options: Managing tenant admins could access sensitive security data from client tenants if delegations are too broad
Sentinel cannot function across tenants
KQL queries do not work across multiple workspaces
Azure Lighthouse does not support Log Analytics workspaces
questions.explanation: Azure Lighthouse delegations grant the managing tenant access to resources in client tenants. If the delegation is too broad (e.g., Owner or Contributor on the Sentinel resource group), managing tenant admins could access all security data. Follow least privilege for delegations.

questions.question: A playbook triggered by a Sentinel automation rule fails intermittently with '403 Forbidden' errors when calling the Microsoft Graph API. The playbook uses a managed identity. What should you check?
options: Whether the managed identity has the required Microsoft Graph API permissions and whether admin consent has been granted
Whether the playbook's Logic App is in the same region as Sentinel
Whether the Logic App has a premium connector license
Whether the KQL query in the analytics rule has syntax errors
questions.explanation: Logic App playbooks using managed identities need explicit API permissions granted and admin-consented for Microsoft Graph operations. A 403 error indicates the identity lacks the required permissions or consent was not granted.

questions.question: You are developing custom hunting queries in Sentinel. Your query needs to detect data exfiltration by identifying users who downloaded an unusually large amount of data compared to their baseline. Which KQL technique should you use?
options: Use series_decompose_anomalies() or percentile calculations to compare each user's download volume against their historical baseline
Set a fixed threshold of 1 GB for all users
Count total downloads per user without considering historical patterns
Use the take operator to sample random download events
questions.explanation: The series_decompose_anomalies() function or statistical functions like percentile detect deviations from each user's individual baseline. A fixed threshold generates false positives for high-volume users and misses low-volume users who suddenly spike.

questions.question: Your organization acquires a company that uses Splunk for SIEM. You need to integrate their existing detection rules into Microsoft Sentinel. What is the most efficient approach?
options: Use the Sentinel migration tool to convert Splunk SPL detection rules to KQL analytics rules
Manually rewrite every Splunk rule in KQL from scratch
Run both Splunk and Sentinel indefinitely in parallel
Export Splunk data to CSV and import it as Sentinel watchlists
questions.explanation: Microsoft provides a SIEM migration experience in Sentinel that can translate Splunk SPL detection rules to equivalent KQL analytics rules, significantly reducing the migration effort compared to manual rewriting.

questions.question: You need to detect credential theft via OS credential dumping (like Mimikatz) on Windows servers monitored by Sentinel. Which combination of data sources and detection gives the best coverage?
options: Windows Security Events (process creation with Sysmon or Event ID 4688) plus Defender for Endpoint alerts correlated in Sentinel
Azure Activity Log monitoring only
NSG flow logs analysis
Azure AD sign-in logs only
questions.explanation: Credential dumping occurs at the OS level. Windows Security Events (process creation, especially with Sysmon) capture the execution of tools like Mimikatz. Combining this with Defender for Endpoint behavioral alerts in Sentinel provides layered detection.

questions.question: Your Sentinel workspace has 200+ analytics rules. Several rules reference the same threat intelligence IoCs hardcoded in the KQL queries. When the threat landscape changes, updating each rule is time-consuming. How should you optimize this?
options: Use the Threat Intelligence platform data connector and TI mapping analytics rules that automatically match IoCs against all log data
Create a single analytics rule that checks all IoCs
Store IoCs in an Azure Storage blob and reference it in each query
Disable all rules and rely on manual hunting
questions.explanation: The Threat Intelligence connector ingests IoCs centrally. TI mapping analytics rules automatically correlate these IoCs against log data across tables, eliminating the need to hardcode IoCs in individual rules and simplifying updates.

questions.question: You need to retain Sentinel security data for 2 years for compliance but only need to actively query the last 90 days. What is the most cost-effective configuration?
options: Set interactive retention to 90 days and total retention (archive) to 2 years in the Log Analytics workspace
Set interactive retention to 2 years for all tables
Export all data to Azure Blob Storage daily using Logic Apps
Create a second Sentinel workspace for archived data
questions.explanation: Log Analytics allows you to set interactive retention (full query capability) separately from total retention (archive tier, lower cost, limited queries). Setting 90 days interactive and 2 years total optimizes costs while meeting compliance requirements.

questions.question: You want to proactively search for threats in Sentinel that existing analytics rules may not detect. Which feature should you use?
options: Hunting queries (using built-in or custom KQL queries to search for suspicious patterns)
Workbooks
Data connector health monitoring
Content Hub solutions
questions.explanation: Hunting in Sentinel allows security analysts to proactively search for threats using KQL queries before analytics rules trigger. It includes built-in queries aligned with MITRE ATT&CK and supports custom query creation.

questions.question: You are designing a Sentinel deployment for a large enterprise with workloads across Azure, AWS, and GCP. How should you architect the multi-cloud data collection?
options: Use native Sentinel multi-cloud connectors for AWS (S3/CloudTrail) and GCP (Pub/Sub) alongside Azure connectors, feeding into a single Sentinel workspace
Deploy separate Sentinel workspaces for each cloud provider
Only collect Azure data and rely on each cloud's native SIEM for the others
Use a third-party SIEM for multi-cloud and forward alerts to Sentinel
questions.explanation: Sentinel provides native data connectors for AWS (CloudTrail, S3, GuardDuty) and GCP (Pub/Sub, Security Command Center). A single workspace with multi-cloud connectors provides unified visibility, correlation, and incident management across all three clouds.
